20.7: If he says, "It is good, your servant can be at peace," but if he becomes angry, know that he has truly resolved to do evil.
20.8: Therefore, show kindness to your servant, since it was by a covenant in the name of the Lord that you drew your servant to yourself. If there is any fault in me, kill me yourself, for why should you bring me to your father?»
20.9: Jonathan said to him, "Far be it from you to think that. For if I learn that my father has resolved to harm you, I swear I will tell you."«
20.10: David said to Jonathan, "Who will tell me about this, or about what your father's response would be disastrous?"«
20.11: Jonathan said to David, "Come, let's go out into the fields," and they both went out into the fields.
20.12: Jonathan said to David, «The Lord, the God of Israel, I will sound out my father tomorrow or the day after, and if things are going well for David and I do not send word to you,
20.13: May the Lord deal with Jonathan in all his severity. If it pleases my father to harm you, I will also inform you and send you away, so that you may go in peace and the Lord may be with you, as he was with my father.
20.14: And if I am still alive, may the Lord show me kindness, and if I die,
20.15: "Do not ever remove your goodness from my house, not even when the Lord cuts off from the face of the earth every one of David's enemies."»
20.16: This is how Jonathan made an alliance with the house of David, and how the Lord took revenge on David's enemies.
20.17: Jonathan once again implored David by the love he had for him, for he loved him as his own soul.
20.18: Jonathan told him, "Tomorrow is the new moon, we will notice that your place is empty.
20.19: On the third day, you shall go down quickly and come to the place where you were hiding on the day of the incident, and you shall remain near the stone of Ezel.
20.20: I will shoot three arrows towards the side of the stone, as if I were aiming at a target.
20.21: And behold, I will send the boy, saying to him, Go, find the arrows. If I say to the boy, Behold, the arrows are behind you, take them, then come, for all is well with you and there is no danger, the Lord lives.
20.22: But if I say to the boy, “Look, the arrows are before you; go away, for the Lord is sending you away.”.
20.23: And as for the word that you and I have spoken, behold, the Lord is between you and me forever.»
20.24: David hid in the fields. When the new moon arrived, the king took his place at the feast to eat.,
20.25: As usual, the king sat on his seat, on the seat that was near the wall, Jonathan stood up and Abner sat next to Saul and David's place remained empty.
20.26: Saul said nothing that day, for, he said, "something has happened to him; he is not pure, certainly he is not pure."«
20.27: The next day, the second day of the new moon, David's place was still empty, and Saul said to Jonathan, his son, "Why didn't Jesse's son come to the meal yesterday or today?"«
20.28: Jonathan replied to Saul: "David has earnestly asked me for permission to go to Bethlehem.
20.29: He said, »Please let me go, for we have a family sacrifice in the city, and my brother has commanded me to attend. If I have found favor in your eyes, please let me go and see my brothers.” That is why he did not come to the king’s table.»
20.30: Then Saul's anger burned against Jonathan, and he said to him, "Son of a perverse and rebellious woman, do I not know that you have made the son of Jesse your friend, to your shame and to the shame of your mother's nakedness?
20.31: For as long as the son of Jesse lives on the earth, there will be no security for you or your kingdom. And now send for him and bring him to me, for he is a son of death.»
20.32: Jonathan answered Saul, his father, and said to him, «Why should he be put to death? What has he done?»
20.33: And Saul raised his spear against him to strike him. Jonathan understood that it was decided with his father to kill David.
20.34: So Jonathan got up from the table in a violent rage and did not eat anything on the second day of the new moon, because he was grieved on account of David, because his father had insulted him.
20.35: The next morning, Jonathan went to the fields, as agreed with David, accompanied by a little boy.
20.36: He said to his boy, "Run, find the arrows I'm going to shoot." The boy ran, and Jonathan shot an arrow in such a way as to overtake him.
20.37: When the boy arrived at the spot where Jonathan had shot the arrow, Jonathan shouted at the boy and said, "Isn't the arrow farther away than you?"«
20.38: Jonathan shouted again at the boy: "Quickly, hurry, don't stop." And Jonathan's boy picked up the arrow and returned to his master.
20.39: The boy knew nothing; only Jonathan and David understood.
20.40: Jonathan gave his weapons to the boy who was with him and said, "Go and take them to the city."«
20.41: As soon as the boy had left, David got up from the south side and, throwing himself face down on the ground, he prostrated himself three times before Jonathan, then they kissed each other and wept for each other, until David burst into tears.
20.42: And Jonathan said to David, «Go in peace, now that we have both sworn an oath in the name of the Lord, saying, »The Lord be between me and you, and between my descendants and your descendants forever.’”

21.1: David got up and left, and Jonathan went back into the city.
21.2: David went to Nobah, to the high priest Ahimelech, and Ahimelech ran up to David in fear and said to him, "Why are you alone and is there no one with you?"«
21.3: David answered the priest Ahimelech: «The king gave me an order and said: Let no one know anything about the matter for which I am sending you and I have given you an order. I have assigned my men this meeting place.
21.4: "And now, what do you have on hand? Give me five loaves of bread, or whatever you can find."»
21.5: The priest answered David, saying, "I do not have ordinary bread on hand, but there is consecrated bread, provided that your people have abstained from women."«
21.6: David answered the priest and said, «We have abstained from women for the three days since I left, and the bodies of my men are pure, even though this is a profane expedition. How much more so are they today in a state of holiness.»
21.7: Then the priest gave him consecrated bread, for there was no other bread there except the bread of the Presence, which had been taken away from before the Lord, to be replaced with fresh bread at the time it was taken away.
21.8: That same day, there was a man there from among Saul’s servants, detained before the Lord; his name was Doeg, an Edomite, chief of Saul’s shepherds.
21.9: David said to Ahimelech, «Do you not have a spear or a sword with you? For I have not even brought my sword or my weapons with me, because the king’s command was urgent.»
21.10: The priest replied, «Here is the sword of Goliath the Philistine, whom you killed in the Valley of Elah; it is wrapped in the cloak, behind the ephod. If you want it, take it, for there is no other here.» And David said, «There is no like it; give it to me.»
21.11: David got up and fled that same day from Saul; he went to Achish, king of Geth.
21.12: Achish's servants said to him, "Isn't this David, king of the land? Isn't this the one about whom they sang and danced: 'Saul has killed his thousands, and David his ten thousand'?"«
21.13: David took these words to heart and was very afraid of Achish, king of Geth.
21.14: He concealed his reason from their eyes and played the fool in their hands, he beat the drum on the door panels and let his saliva drip onto his beard.
21.15: Achis said to his servants, "You can see that this man is a madman, why did you bring him to me?"
21.16: Do I lack enough madmen that you should bring me this one to act madly in front of me? Must he enter my house?»

22.1: David left there and fled to the cave of Odollam. When his brothers and all his father's household heard about it, they went down to him there.
22.2: All the oppressed, all those who had creditors, and all those who were bitter, gathered around him, and he became their leader; there were about four hundred men with him.
22.3: From there, David went to Mizpah of Moab. He said to the king of Moab, «Please let my father and mother stay with you until I know what God will do for me.»
22.4: And he brought them before the king of Moab, and they stayed with him as long as David was in the stronghold.
22.5: The prophet Gad said to David, «Do not remain in the stronghold; go and return to the land of Judah.» So David went and went to the forest of Hareth.
22.6: Saul learned that David and the people who were with him had been recognized. Now Saul was sitting at Gibeah under the tamarisk tree on the hill, with his spear in his hand, and all his servants were lined up before him.
22.7: Saul said to his servants who were lined up before him, «Listen, Benjamites: will the son of Jesse also give fields and vineyards to all of you, and make all of you commanders of thousands and commanders of hundreds,
22.8: Why have you all conspired against me? Is it because no one has informed me that my son has made a pact with the son of Jesse, and that none of you cares for me or warns me that my son has stirred up my servant against me to set traps for me, as he is doing today?»
22.9: Doeg the Edomite, who was the chief of Saul's servants, answered and said, "I saw the son of Jesse come to Nobah, to Ahimelech, the son of Ahitob.
22.10: Ahimelech consulted the Lord on his behalf and gave him provisions; he also gave him the sword of Goliath the Philistine.»
22.11: The king sent for Ahimelech the priest, son of Ahitob, and all his father's household, the priests who were at Nobah.
22.12: They all came to the king, and Saul said, "Listen, son of Ahitob." He replied, "Here I am, my lord."«
22.13: Saul said to him, «Why have you and the son of Jesse conspired against me? You gave him bread and a sword and inquired of God for him, only to have him rise up against me and lie in wait for me, as he is doing today?»
22.14: Ahimelech answered the king and said, «Which of all your servants is like David, faithful and proven, the king’s son-in-law, admitted to your councils and honored in your house?
22.15: Would I have begun to consult God on his behalf today? Far be it from me. Let not the king place a burden on his servant that would weigh heavily upon my father's entire household, for your servant has known nothing of all this, neither little nor much.»
22.16: The king said, "You will die, Ahimelech, you and all your father's house."«
22.17: And the king said to the guards who stood near him, «Turn around and put the priests of the Lord to death, for their hand is with David, and knowing full well that he was in flight, they did not inform me.» But the king’s servants would not raise their hands to strike the priests of the Lord.
22.18: Then the king said to Doeg, «Turn around and strike down the priests.» So Doeg the Edomite turned around and struck down the priests, killing eighty-five men that day who wore the linen ephod.
22.19: Saul struck again with the edge of the sword Nobah, the priestly city: men and women, children and infants, oxen, donkeys and sheep, were put to the edge of the sword.
22.20: Only one son of Ahimelech, son of Ahitob, escaped; his name was Abiathar, and he took refuge with David.
22.21: Abiathar told David that Saul had killed the priests of the Lord.
22.22: And David said to Abiathar, «I knew that day that Doeg the Edomite, who was there, would not fail to inform Saul. I am the one responsible for the death of your entire father’s house.”.
22.23: Stay with me, do not be afraid, for whoever seeks my life seeks your life, and with me you will be safe.»

23.1: David was told, "Look, the Philistines are attacking Ceilah and plundering the threshing floors."«
23.2: David inquired of the Lord, saying, «Shall I go and defeat these Philistines?» And the Lord answered David, «Go, defeat the Philistines and rescue Ceilah.»
23.3: But David's men said to him, "Behold, we are afraid in Judah; how much more so if we go to Ceilah against the Philistine troops?"«
23.4: David inquired of the Lord again, and the Lord answered him, saying, «Get up, go down to Ceilah, for I will deliver the Philistines into your hand.»
23.5: So David went with his men to Ceilah and attacked the Philistines, capturing their livestock and inflicting a great defeat upon them. Thus David delivered the inhabitants of Ceilah.
23.6: Now when Abiathar, son of Ahimelech, fled to David at Ceilah, he went down with the ephod in his hand.
23.7: Saul was told that David had gone to Ceilah, and Saul said, "God has delivered him into my hands, for he has shut himself in by coming to a city that has gates and bars."«
23.8: And Saul summoned all the people to war, in order to go down to Ceilah and besiege David and his men.
23.9: But David, having learned that Saul was plotting evil against him, said to the priest Abiathar, "Bring the ephod."«
23.10: And David said, «The Lord, God of Israel, your servant, has heard that Saul is trying to come to Ceilah to destroy the city because of me.
23.11: Will the inhabitants of Ceilah deliver me into his hands? Will Saul come down, as your servant has heard? Lord, God of Israel, please reveal it to your servant.» The Lord answered, «He will come down.»
23.12: And David said, «Will the inhabitants of Ceilah hand me and my men over to Saul?» The Lord answered, «They will hand you over.»
23.13: So David and his men, about six hundred in number, set out from Ceilah and wandered about aimlessly. When Saul heard that David had fled from Ceilah, he halted his march.
23.14: David remained in the desert, in the refuges, and he stayed in the mountains in the desert of Ziph. Saul searched for him every day, but God did not give him into his hands.
23.15: David learned that Saul had set out to kill him: David was standing in the wilderness of Ziph, in the forest,
23.16: And Jonathan, son of Saul, arose and went to David in the forest. He strengthened his hand in God and said to him:
23.17: «Do not be afraid, for the hand of Saul, my father, will not touch you. You will reign over Israel, and I will be second only to you; Saul, my father, knows this well.»
23.18: They both made a covenant before the Lord, and David stayed in the forest while Jonathan returned home.
23.19: The Ziphites went up to Saul at Gibeah and said, «David is hiding among us in refuges in the forest, on the hill of Hailah, which is south of the heath.
23.20: "Come down then, O king, as your whole soul desires; it is for us to deliver him into the king's hands."»
23.21: Saul said, «Blessed are you of the Lord, for you have had mercy on me.”.
23.22: Go, I beg you, make sure again, find out and see where he is going and who has seen him there, for I am told he is very cunning.
23.23: See and learn all the hiding places where he is, then come back to me with reliable information and I will go with you. If he is in the land, I will search for him among all the thousands of Judah.»
23.24: So they got up and went to Ziph before Saul. But David and his men had withdrawn to the wilderness of Maon, in the plain, south of the moor.
23.25: Saul and his men went in search of David. When David learned of this, he went down to the rock and stayed in the wilderness of Maon. Saul was informed of this and pursued David into the wilderness of Maon.,
23.26: Saul was walking on one side of the mountain and David with his men on the other side of the mountain, David was hurrying to escape from Saul, while Saul and his men surrounded David and his men to capture them.
23.27: A messenger came to Saul, saying, «Hurry and come, for the Philistines have invaded the land.»
23.28: Saul stopped pursuing David and went to meet the Philistines. That is why the place was called Sela-Hammahlekoth.

24.1: David went up from there and settled in the refuges of Engaddi.
24.2: When Saul returned from pursuing the Philistines, he was told, "David is in the wilderness of Engaddi."«
24.3: Saul took three thousand elite men from all Israel and went in search of David and his people as far as the rocks of the wild goats.
24.4: He came to the sheep pens which were near the road, there was a cave there, where Saul went in to relieve himself and David and his men were sitting at the back of the cave.
24.5: David's men said to him, "This is the day the Lord spoke of: 'I will deliver your enemy into your hands; do to him as you please.'" David got up and secretly cut off a corner of Saul's robe.
24.6: After this, David's heart swelled because he had cut off a corner of Saul's robe.
24.7: And he said to his men, «The Lord forbid that I should do such a thing as lay a hand on my master, the Lord’s anointed, for he is the Lord’s anointed.»
24.8: With his words, David restrained his men and prevented them from attacking Saul. Saul got up to leave the cave and continued on his way.
24.9: After this David got up and, coming out of the cave, he began to shout after Saul, saying, "O king, my lord." Saul looked behind him, and David bowed his face to the ground and prostrated himself.
24.10: And David said to Saul, «Why do you listen to the words of people who say, ‘Behold, David seeks to harm you?’”
24.11: Behold, today your eyes have seen how the Lord delivered you, this very day, into my hands in the cave. They told me to kill you, but my eye was moved by your pity, and I said, I will not lay a hand on my lord, for he is the Lord’s anointed.
24.12: Look, my father, look in my hand at the corner of your cloak. Since I cut off the corner of your cloak and did not kill you, acknowledge and see that there is no wickedness or rebellion in my conduct, and that I have not sinned against you. And you are hunting me down to take my life.
24.13: May the Lord judge between you and me, and may the Lord avenge me on you. But my hand will not be on you.
24.14: From wickedness comes wickedness, says the old proverb, so my hand will not be on you.
24.15: Whom did the king of Israel set out after? Who are you chasing? A dead dog? A flea?
24.16: May the Lord judge and decide between you and me. May he look upon me and plead my case, and may his judgment deliver me from your hand.»
24.17: When David had finished speaking these words to Saul, Saul said, "Is that your voice, my son David?" And Saul raised his voice and wept.
24.18: He said to David, «You are more righteous than I, for you have done me good and I have repaid you evil.”.
24.19: Today you have shown that you are acting kindly towards me, since the Lord has delivered me into your hands and you have not killed me.
24.20: If someone meets their enemy, do they let them go on their way in peace? May the Lord reward you for what you have done for me this day.
24.21: Now I know that you will be king and that the kingdom of Israel will be firmly established in your hands.
24.22: Swear to me by the Lord that you will not destroy my descendants after me and that you will not blot out my name from my father's house.»
24.23: David swore to Saul. And Saul went to his house, and David and his men went up to their refuge.

25.1: However, Samuel died, and all Israel gathered together, mourned for him, and buried him in his home at Ramah. Then David arose and went down to the wilderness of Paran.
25.2: There was a man in Maon whose property was in Carmel; he was a very rich man, he had three thousand sheep and a thousand goats, and he was in Carmel for the shearing of his sheep.
25.3: The man's name was Nabal, and his wife's name was Abigail. The woman was intelligent and beautiful, but the man was harsh and wicked in his deeds; he was of the lineage of Caleb.
25.4: David learned in the desert that Nabal was shearing his sheep.
25.5: David sent ten young men and said to them, «Go up to Carmel and find Nabal, and greet him in my name.”
25.6: And you shall speak to him thus: For life. Peace be with you, peace be with your house, and peace be with all that belongs to you.
25.7: And now I have learned that you have shearers. But your shepherds were with us; we caused them no trouble, and nothing of the flock was taken from them during all the time they were at Carmel.
25.8: Ask your servants, and they will tell you. May the young men find favor in your eyes, since we come in a day of joy. Give, I pray you, to your servants and to your son David whatever your hand can find.»
25.9: When David's young men arrived, they repeated all these words to Nabal in David's name and then they rested.
25.10: Nabal answered David's servants and said, "Who is David and who is the son of Jesse? Many are the servants who run away from their masters these days.
25.11: And I would take my bread and my water and my livestock that I slaughtered for my shearers and give them to people who come from I don't know where?»
25.12: David's young men retraced their steps and returned, and when they arrived, they repeated all these words to him.
25.13: Then David said to his men, "Each of you should take his sword." And they each took his sword, and David also took his sword. About four hundred men went up after David, and two hundred remained near the baggage.
25.14: One of Nabal's servants came and brought the news to Abigail, saying, "Behold, David has sent messengers from the desert to greet our master, who has fallen upon them.
25.15: And yet these people were good to us, they caused us no pain and nothing was taken from us during the whole time we traveled with them, when we were in the countryside.
25.16: They served as our shield, both night and day, the whole time we were with them, grazing the flocks.
25.17: "Now recognize and see what you must do, for the evil has been determined against our master and all his house; for him, he is a son of Belial, and no one can speak to him."»
25.18: Immediately Abigail took two hundred loaves of bread, two skins of wine, five prepared sheep, five measures of roasted grain, one hundred clusters of raisins, and two hundred dried figs, and, having placed them on donkeys,
25.19: She said to her young men, "Go on ahead of me, I'll follow you." But she said nothing to Nabal, her husband.
25.20: As she was going down, riding on a donkey, to a sheltered spot on the mountain, David and his men were coming down opposite her, and she met them.
25.21: David said, "It is in vain that I have watched over all that this man has in the desert, and nothing has been taken from all that he possesses; he repays me evil for good.".
25.22: May God deal with David's enemies in all his severity. Of all that belongs to Nabal, I will leave nothing alive until daybreak, not even a single male.»
25.23: As soon as Abigail saw David, she quickly got off the donkey and, falling on her face before David, she prostrated herself on the ground.
25.24: Then she fell at his feet, saying, «Let the blame be on me, my lord, on me. Allow your servant to speak in your ear and listen to the words of your servant.”.
25.25: My lord should pay no attention to that man from Belial, Nabal, for he is exactly what his name indicates: his name is The Madman, and madness is with him. But I, your servant, have not seen the men of my lord whom you sent.
25.26: Now, my lord, as surely as the Lord lives and as your soul lives, the Lord has preserved you from shedding blood and taking revenge with your own hand. And now, may your enemies and those who wish harm upon my lord be like Nabal.
25.27: Therefore, accept this gift that your servant brings to my lord, and let it be distributed to the young men who follow my lord.
25.28: Please forgive your servant’s fault, for the Lord will surely make my lord a stable house, for my lord supports the Lord’s wars and no evil will be found in you all the days of your life.
25.29: If anyone rises up to pursue you and seek your life, the life of my lord will be carefully preserved with the lives of the living with the Lord your God, and the life of your enemies he will sling away.
25.30: When the Lord has done to my lord according to all the good he has spoken about you, and has appointed you ruler over Israel,
25.31: It will not be a cause of remorse for you, nor will it be a source of grief for my lord, that he shed blood without cause and took revenge himself. And when the Lord has dealt well with my lord, remember your servant.»
25.32: David said to Abigail, «Blessed be the Lord, the God of Israel, who has sent you to meet me today. Blessed be your great wisdom,
25.33: And blessed be you, you who prevented me this day from shedding blood and taking revenge with my own hand.
25.34: Otherwise, as surely as the Lord, the God of Israel, lives, who has kept me from harming you, if you had not hurried to meet me, Nabal would have had nothing left by daybreak, not even a single male.»
25.35: David took from Abigail what she had brought him and said to her, «Go up in peace to your house; see, I have listened to your voice and lifted up your countenance.»
25.36: Abigail returned to Nabal, and behold, he was holding a feast in his house, like a king's feast, and Nabal's heart was joyful and he was completely drunk. She taught him neither little nor much until daybreak.
25.37: But in the morning, when Nabal came back from his drunkenness, his wife told him these things and his heart received a mortal blow and he became like a stone.
25.38: About ten days later, the Lord struck Nabal and he died.
25.39: When David heard of Nabal's death, he said, "Blessed be the Lord, who has pleaded my cause and avenged me against the insult I received from Nabal's hand, and who has kept his servant from doing evil. The Lord has brought Nabal's wickedness back on his own head." Then David sent word to Abigail that he wished to take her as his wife.
25.40: When they arrived at Abigail's house in Carmel, David's servants spoke to her, saying, "David has sent us to you, to take you as his wife."«
25.41: She got up and, prostrating herself with her face to the ground, said, "Behold, your servant is like a slave to wash the feet of my lord's servants."«
25.42: Abigail immediately got up and mounted a donkey, and five of her daughters followed her; she went with David’s messengers and became his wife.
25.43: David also took Ahinoam of Jezreel, and both became his wives.
25.44: But Saul had given his daughter Michal, David's wife, to Phalti of Gallim, son of Laish.

26.1: The Ziphites came to Saul at Gibeah and said, "David is hiding on the hill of Hailah, east of the desert."«
26.2: Saul arose and went down to the desert of Ziph with three thousand elite men of Israel, to seek David in the desert of Ziph.
26.3: Saul camped on the hill of Hailah, east of the desert, near the road, while David remained in the wilderness. When David saw that Saul was searching for him in the wilderness,
26.4: David sent spies and learned that Saul had actually arrived.
26.5: David got up and came to the place where Saul was encamped. David saw the place where Saul was lying, with Abner, son of Ner, commander of his army: now Saul was lying in the middle of the camp and the people were encamped around him.
26.6: So David addressed Ahimelech the Hittite and Abishai, son of Zeruiah and brother of Joab, saying, «Who will go down with me to the camp to Saul?» And Abishai replied, «I will go down with you.»
26.7: David and Abishai came to the people by night and behold, Saul was lying in the middle of the camp and was asleep, with his spear stuck in the ground at his head, and Abner and the people were lying around him.
26.8: Abishai said to David, «Today God has imprisoned your enemy in your hands; now, I pray you, let me strike him with the spear and pin him to the ground with one blow, without having to return.»
26.9: But David said to Abishai, «Do not kill him. For who could lay a hand on the Lord’s anointed and go unpunished?»
26.10: And David said, «As surely as the Lord lives, it will be the Lord who strikes him down; either his day will come and he will die, or he will go down to war and he will perish.”,
26.11: But the Lord forbid that I should lay a hand on the Lord's anointed. Now take the spear that is at his head, along with the jug of water, and let us go.»
26.12: And David took the spear and the jug of water that were at Saul's head, and they went away. No one saw, no one knew, no one woke up, for they were all asleep, because the Lord had put a deep sleep upon them.
26.13: David crossed to the other side and stood far away on the mountaintop, a large space separated them.
26.14: And David called out to the people and to Abner son of Ner, saying, «Will you not answer, Abner?» Abner answered, saying, «Who are you, crying out to the king?»
26.15: David said to Abner, «Are you not a man? And who is your equal in Israel? Why then have you not guarded the king, your lord? For one of the people has come to kill the king, your lord.
26.16: What you have done is wrong. As surely as the Lord lives, you have deserved death for not guarding your master, the Lord's anointed. Now look where the king's spear and the water jug that was at his bedside are.»
26.17: Saul recognized David's voice and said, "Is that your voice, my son David?" And David replied, "It is my voice, O king, my lord."«
26.18: He added, "Why is my lord pursuing his servant? What have I done, and what crime has my hand committed?"
26.19: Now let the king, my lord, deign to listen to the words of your servant: if it is the Lord who stirs you up against me, let him accept the incense of an offering; but if it is men, let them be cursed before the Lord, since they have now driven me out, to take away my place from the inheritance of the Lord, saying: Go serve foreign gods.
26.20: And now may my blood not fall upon the earth, far from the face of the Lord. For the king of Israel has gone out to look for a flea, as one would chase a partridge in the mountains.»
26.21: Saul said, «I have sinned; return, my son David, for I will not harm you again, since on this day my life was precious in your eyes. Behold, I have acted foolishly and have made a great mistake.»
26.22: David replied, "Here is the spear, O king; let one of your young men come and take it.".
26.23: The Lord will repay each person according to their justice and faithfulness, for the Lord had delivered you into my hands today, and I would not lay a hand on the Lord's anointed.
26.24: »Just as your life has been precious in my eyes today, so my life will be precious in the eyes of the Lord, and he will deliver me from all distress.”
26.25: Saul said to David, "Blessed be you, my son David. You will certainly succeed in your endeavors." David continued on his way, and Saul returned home.

27.1: David said to himself, "I will one day perish by the hand of Saul; there is nothing better for me than to flee quickly to the land of the Philistines, so that Saul will give up searching for me any longer in all the territory of Israel, and I will escape his hand."«
27.2: And David arose, he and the six hundred men who were with him, and they went over to Achish, son of Maoch, king of Geth.
27.3: David remained with Achish in Geth, he and his people, each with his family, and David with his two wives, Ahinoam of Jezreel and Abigail of Carmel, wife of Nabal.
27.4: Saul was told that David had fled to Geth, and he did not resume his pursuit.
27.5: David said to Achish, «If I have found favor in your eyes, let me be given a place to live in one of the towns in the countryside, for why should your servant live with you in the royal city?»
27.6: And on that day Achish gave him Ziklag, and that is why Ziklag has belonged to the kings of Judah to this day.
27.7: The number of days that David spent in the land of the Philistines was one year and four months.
27.8: David and his men went up and raided the Gessurians, the Gerzians and the Amalekites, for these peoples had lived from ancient times in the region of Sur and as far as the land of Egypt.
27.9: David ravaged the land, leaving neither man nor woman alive, taking away sheep, oxen, donkeys, camels, and clothing, then he returned to Achish.
27.10: Achish would ask, "Where have you raided today?" And David would reply, "Into the Negev of Judah, into the Negev of the Jerahamelites, and into the Negev of the Kenites."«
27.11: David left no man or woman alive to bring to Geth, for fear, he said to himself, "that they might report against us, saying, 'Thus David did.'" And this was his way of acting all the time he remained in the land of the Philistines.
27.12: Achish trusted David and said, "He has made himself odious to his people, to Israel, therefore he will be my servant forever.

28.1: At that time, the Philistines gathered their troops into a single army to go and fight against Israel. And Achish said to David, «Know that you and your men will come with me to the camp.»
28.2: David answered Achish, "You will see what your servant will do." And Achish said to David, "And I will appoint you as my guardian forever."«
28.3: Samuel had died, all Israel had mourned for him, and he was buried in Ramah, his own city. And Saul had removed from the land those who consulted mediums and spiritists.
28.4: The Philistines having gathered together, came to camp at Shunem, Saul gathered all Israel and they camped at Gilboa.
28.5: When Saul saw the Philistine camp, he was afraid and his heart was greatly troubled.
28.6: Saul inquired of the Lord, but the Lord did not answer him, neither by dreams, nor by the Urim, nor by the prophets.
28.7: Then Saul said to his servants, «Find me a woman who is a medium, so that I may go to her and consult her.» His servants told him, «There is a woman in Endor who is a medium.»
28.8: Saul disguised himself and put on other clothes and left, accompanied by two men. They arrived at the woman's house at night, and Saul said to her, "Predict the future for me by summoning a dead man, and bring up for me the one I name."«
28.9: The woman replied, "Now you know what Saul has done, how he cut off from the land those who summon the dead and the soothsayers, why are you setting a trap for me, to kill me?
28.10: Saul swore to him by the Lord, saying, «As surely as the Lord lives, no harm will come to you because of this.»
28.11: And the woman said, "Who shall I bring up for you?" He replied, "Bring up Samuel for me."«
28.12: When the woman saw Samuel, she cried out loudly and said to Saul, "Why have you deceived me? You are Saul."«
28.13: The king said to her, "Do not be afraid, but what have you seen?" The woman said to Saul, "I see a god coming up out of the earth."«
28.14: He said to her, "What does he look like?" And she replied, "It is an old man coming up, and he is wrapped in a cloak." Saul realized that it was Samuel, and he fell facedown to the ground and prostrated himself.
28.15: Samuel said to Saul, «Why have you troubled me by bringing me up?» Saul replied, «I am in great distress: the Philistines are waging war against me, and God has departed from me; he has not answered me, either by prophets or by dreams. I have summoned you so that you may tell me what I should do.»
28.16: Samuel said, «Why do you consult me, since the Lord has withdrawn from you and become your adversary?
28.17: The Lord has done as he announced through me: the Lord has taken the kingdom from your hand and given it to your companion, to David.
28.18: Because you did not obey the voice of the Lord and did not treat Amalek according to the fierceness of his anger, that is why the Lord has acted thus toward you this day.
28.19: And the Lord will even deliver Israel with you into the hands of the Philistines. Tomorrow, you and your sons will be with me, and the Lord will deliver the camp of Israel into the hands of the Philistines.»
28.20: Saul immediately fell to the ground from his full height, for Samuel's words had filled him with terror, and moreover, his strength failed him, for he had eaten no food all day and all night.
28.21: The woman came to Saul and, seeing his great distress, she said to him, «Your servant has obeyed your voice; I have risked my life, obeying the words that you spoke to me.
28.22: »Now listen to the voice of your servant, and let me offer you a piece of bread; eat it, so that you may have strength as you continue on your way.”
28.23: But he refused and said, «I will not eat.» His servants and the woman urged him, and he yielded to their entreaties. He rose from the ground and sat on the couch.
28.24: The woman had a fattened calf at home; she quickly killed it and, taking some flour, she kneaded it and baked unleavened bread.
28.25: She placed them before Saul and his servants, and they ate. Then they got up and left that same night.

29.1: The Philistines gathered all their troops at Aphek and Israel was encamped near the spring in Jezreel.
29.2: While the Philistine princes advanced at the head of hundreds and thousands, and David and his men marched in the rearguard with Achish,
29.3: The Philistine leaders said, «What are these Hebrews?» Achish answered the Philistine leaders, «Isn’t this David, the servant of Saul, king of Israel, who has been with me for days and years, and I have found nothing wrong with him from the time he came to us until this day?»
29.4: But the Philistine leaders were angry with Achish and said to him, «Send this man back to where you stationed him. Do not let him go down with us to battle, or he will be an enemy to us in the fighting. And how can he regain his master’s favor except by offering him the heads of these men?”
29.5: Isn't this the David for whom they sang while dancing: Saul killed his thousand and David his ten thousand?.
29.6: Achish called David and said to him, «As surely as the Lord lives, you are an upright man, and I look favorably upon all your conduct with me in the camp, for I have found nothing wrong with you from the day you came to me until this day, but you are displeasing in the eyes of the princes.
29.7: "Go back and leave in peace, so as not to do anything displeasing in the eyes of the Philistine princes."»
29.8: David said to Achish, «But what have I done, and what have you found in your servant, from the day I came to you until this day, that I have not gone to fight against the enemies of my lord the king?»
29.9: Achish answered and said to David, «I know that you have been good to me like an angel of God, but the leaders of the Philistines say: he will not go up with us to battle.
29.10: "So get up early, you and the servants of your master who came with you; get up early, and as soon as it is light, depart."»
29.11: David and his men got up early, to leave early in the morning and return to the land of the Philistines, and the Philistines went up to Jezreel.

30.1: When David and his men arrived at Ziklag on the third day, the Amalekites had invaded the Negev and at Ziklag they had attacked and burned it.,
30.2: And they had taken captive the women and all those who were there, both small and great, without killing anyone, and they had taken them away and set off again.
30.3: When David and his people arrived at the city, they saw that it was burned down and that their wives, sons, and daughters had been taken captive.
30.4: And David and the people who were with him raised their voices and wept until they had no more strength to weep.
30.5: David's two wives had also been taken captive, Achinoam of Jezreel and Abigail of Carmel, wife of Nabal.
30.6: David was greatly distressed, for the band of men spoke of stoning him, because all the people were bitter in their souls, each concerning his sons and daughters. But David strengthened his spirit in the Lord his God.
30.7: David said to Abiathar the priest, son of Ahimelech, "Bring me the ephod." Abiathar brought the ephod to David.
30.8: And David inquired of the Lord, saying, «Shall I pursue this band? Shall I overtake them?» The Lord answered him, «Pursue, for you will surely overtake them and deliver them.»
30.9: And David set out, he and the six hundred men who were with him. When they came to the brook Besor, the stragglers stopped.
30.10: And David continued the pursuit with four hundred men; two hundred men had stopped, too tired to cross the Besor stream.
30.11: They found an Egyptian in the fields, whom they brought to David. They gave him bread, which he ate, and they gave him water to drink.,
30.12: They gave him a slice of dried fig cake and two raisin cakes. As soon as he had eaten, his spirits returned to him, for he had not eaten food or drunk water for three days and three nights.
30.13: David said to him, "Whose are you and where are you from?" He replied, "I am an Egyptian slave, in the service of an Amalekite, and for three days my master has abandoned me because I was sick.
30.14: We raided the Negev of the Cerethians, the territory of Judah, and the Negev of Caleb, and we burned Ziklag.»
30.15: David said to him, "Will you lead me to that band of men?" He replied, "Swear to me by the name of God that you will not kill me and that you will not hand me over to my master, and I will lead you to that band of men."«
30.16: When he had led him out, behold, the Amalekites were spread out over all the land, eating, drinking and dancing, because of all the great plunder which they had taken from the land of the Philistines and from the land of Judah.
30.17: David beat them from evening until the following evening and none of them escaped, except for four hundred young men, who fled, riding on camels.
30.18: David saved everything the Amalekites had taken, and David saved his two wives.
30.19: They lacked no one, neither small nor great, neither son nor daughter, nor any part of the plunder, nor anything that had been taken from them: David brought it all back.
30.20: And David took all the flocks and herds, and they set out before the herd, saying, "This is David's plunder."«
30.21: David returned to the two hundred men who had been too tired to follow him and had been left at the Besor brook. They came forward to meet David and the people who were with him. Approaching them, David greeted them warmly.
30.22: All the wicked and vile men among those who had gone with David spoke up and said, «Since they did not come with us, we will not give them any of the spoils we saved, except to each man his wife and children, whom he should take with him and leave.»
30.23: But David said, «Do not do that, my brothers, with what the Lord has given us, for he has protected us and delivered into our hands the band that came against us.
30.24: And who would listen to you in this matter? The share must be the same for the one who went down to battle and for the one who stayed behind with the baggage: they will share it together.»
30.25: It was so from that day forward, and David made it a law and a rule that remains to this day.
30.26: Upon returning to Ziklag, David sent some of the spoils to the elders of Judah, his friends, saying, "Here is a present for you from the spoils of the Lord's enemies."«
30.27: He sent these dispatches to those in Bethel, to those in Ramoth of the Negev, to those in Jether,
30.28: to those of Aroer, to those of Sephamoth, to those of Estamo,
30.29: to those of Rachal, to those of the cities of the Jerahamelites, to those of the cities of the Kenites,
30.30: to those of Arama, to those of Cor-Asan, to those of Athach,
30.31: to those in Hebron and in all the places where David and his people had passed.

31.1: When the Philistines gave battle to Israel, the men of Israel fled before the Philistines and fell mortally wounded on Mount Gilboa.
31.2: The Philistines pursued Saul and his sons, and the Philistines killed Jonathan, Abinadab, and Melchisua, Saul's sons.
31.3: The fighting effort was focused on Saul: the archers having discovered him, he was greatly afraid of the archers.
31.4: Then Saul said to his armor-bearer, "Draw your sword and run me through with it, lest the uncircumcised men come and run me through and insult me." His armor-bearer refused to do it, for he was seized with fear, so Saul took his sword and fell upon it.
31.5: The squire, seeing that Saul was dead, also threw himself on his sword and died with him.
31.6: Thus perished together on that day Saul and his three sons, his armor-bearer and all his men.
31.7: The men of Israel who were on this side of the Plain and those who were on this side of the Jordan, seeing that the children of Israel had fled and that Saul and his sons were dead, abandoned their cities and also fled, and the Philistines came and settled there.
31.8: The next day, the Philistines came to plunder the dead and they found Saul and his three sons lying on Mount Gilboa.
31.9: They cut off his head and took away his armor, then they sent and proclaimed this good news throughout the land of the Philistines, in the temples of their idols and among the people.
31.10: They laid Saul's weapons in the temple of Astarte and fastened his body to the walls of Bethsan.
31.11: The inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead, having learned what the Philistines had done to Saul,
31.12: All the valiant warriors arose and, after marching all night, they removed from the walls of Bethsan the body of Saul and the bodies of his sons, and returned to Jabesh, where they burned them.
31.13: They took their bones and buried them under the tamarisk tree at Jabesh, and they fasted for seven days.
